name: icaap_risk_inventory_materiality_v1.md
description: Comprehensive risk inventory covering six risk categories with sub-risks, gross materiality assessments, methods, and ownership mapping
content:
# 1. Introduction

This document presents a comprehensive Risk Inventory & Materiality Assessment for the ICAAP (Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process). The assessment follows a gross (pre-mitigation) approach, evaluating inherent risk materiality before the application of risk mitigation techniques. All risk types, sub-types, materiality thresholds, assessment methods, and ownership are documented below.

**Basis of Assessment:**
- Gross approach: Inherent risk level before mitigation/controls
- Scale: Low / Medium-Low / Medium / Medium-High / High
- Horizon: Current state assessment (point-in-time with forward-looking overlays)

---

# 2. Risk Inventory & Materiality Matrix

## 2.1 Credit Risk

| Sub-Risk | Description | Gross Materiality | Method | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wholesale Credit Risk** | Risk of loss from default of corporate, financial institution, or sovereign counterparties | High | Internal Ratings-Based (IRB) models; PD/LGD/EAD estimation; Probability of Default scoring; Transition matrices | Head of Credit Risk |
| **Retail Credit Risk** | Risk of loss from non-performance of retail borrowers (mortgages, consumer loans, credit cards) | Medium-High | Scorecards; Vintage analysis; Roll-rate models; Behavioral scoring | Head of Retail Credit |
| **Commercial Real Estate (CRE)** | Risk from lending secured by commercial real estate assets | Medium-High | LTV monitoring; Debt-service coverage ratios; Market vacancy/rent analysis | Head of Real Estate Lending |
| **Sovereign Credit Risk** | Risk of default or restructuring by sovereign/government entities | Medium-Low | Country risk scoring; CDS spreads; External rating benchmarks; Sovereign ceiling analysis | Head of Country Risk |
| **Counterparty Credit Risk (CCR)** | Risk that counterparty defaults before final settlement of transactions | Medium | Current Exposure Method (CEM); Standardized Approach for CCR (SA-CCR); PFE modeling; Collateral valuation | Head of Counterparty Risk |
| **Settlement Risk** | Risk of failure in payment/settlement systems | Low | Settlement exposure metrics; DvP/PvP analysis; Nostro/vostro reconciliation | Head of Operations |
| **Concentration Risk (Credit)** | Risk from concentrated exposure to single names, sectors, or geographies | High | Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI); Top-20 exposures; Sector/GICS concentration limits; Granularity adjustment | Head of Credit Risk / Head of Risk Analytics |

## 2.2 Market Risk & CVA

| Sub-Risk | Description | Gross Materiality | Method | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Interest Rate Risk in Trading Book** | Risk from adverse movements in interest rates affecting trading positions | Medium | VaR (parametric/historical); Expected Shortfall (ES); Sensitivity analysis (DV01, key-rate durations); Stress testing | Head of Market Risk |
| **Equity Price Risk** | Risk from adverse movements in equity and equity-derivative positions | Medium-Low | VaR/ES; Factor models; Beta-adjusted sensitivities; Stress scenarios | Head of Market Risk |
| **Foreign Exchange (FX) Risk** | Risk from adverse FX rate movements affecting open positions | Medium | VaR; FX delta/gamma sensitivities; Net open position limits; Stress scenarios | Head of Market Risk |
| **Commodity Risk** | Risk from adverse commodity price movements | Low (if immaterial) | VaR; Commodity Greeks; Basis risk analysis | Head of Market Risk |
| **Credit Valuation Adjustment (CVA) Risk** | Risk of losses from changes in counterparty credit spreads affecting derivatives valuations | Medium | CVA VaR; Spread sensitivity analysis; Right-way/wrong-way risk analysis; CCR-CVA stress testing | Head of Counterparty Risk / Head of Market Risk |
| **Debt Valuation Adjustment (DVA)** | Risk from changes in own credit spreads affecting liability valuations | Low | DVA sensitivity analysis; Own-credit spread monitoring | Head of Treasury / Head of Market Risk |

## 2.3 Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book (IRRBB)

| Sub-Risk | Description | Gross Materiality | Method | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gap Risk** | Risk from maturity/repricing mismatches between assets, liabilities, and off-balance sheet items | Medium-High | Earnings-at-Risk (EaR); Net Interest Income (NII) simulation; Repricing gap analysis; Bucketed gap reports | Head of ALM / Treasurer |
| **Basis Risk** | Risk from imperfect correlation between different reference rates (e.g., SOFR vs. EURIBOR) | Medium | Basis risk sensitivity analysis; NII scenario simulation under different rate paths | Head of ALM |
| **Yield Curve Risk** | Risk from non-parallel shifts in the yield curve | Medium | NII simulation under parallel/non-parallel shocks; Economic Value of Equity (EVE) analysis | Head of ALM |
| **Optionality Risk** | Risk from embedded options (prepayment, early withdrawal, caps/floors) | Medium | Option-adjusted spread (OAS) analysis; Prepayment models; Behavioral assumption modeling (NMD decay, mortgage prepayment) | Head of ALM / Head of Retail Banking |
| **Short Rate / Repricing Risk** | Risk of adverse changes in short-term interest rates affecting repricing frequency mismatches | Medium-High | Gap analysis (cumulative repricing gaps); NII sensitivity to +/- 100/200bp parallel shocks | Head of ALM |

## 2.4 Operational Risk

| Sub-Risk | Description | Gross Materiality | Method | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal Fraud** | Losses from internal fraudulent acts (e.g., unauthorized activity, misappropriation) | Medium | Loss data collection (internal/external); Scenario analysis; Control self-assessments (RCSA) | Head of Operational Risk / Head of Internal Audit |
| **External Fraud** | Losses from external fraudulent acts (e.g., cyber fraud, theft, check fraud) | Medium-High | Loss data analysis; Fraud monitoring systems; Cyber risk quantification; External threat intelligence | Head of Fraud Risk / CISO |
| **Employment Practices & Workplace Safety** | Losses from employment law breaches, discrimination, worker safety | Low | RCSA; Incident tracking; HR compliance reviews | Head of HR / Head of Operational Risk |
| **Clients, Products & Business Practices** | Losses from fiduciary breaches, mis-selling, improper business practices | Medium | RCSA; Regulatory breach tracking; Product approval reviews; Conduct risk monitoring | Head of Compliance / Head of Operational Risk |
| **Business Disruption & System Failures** | Losses from system outages, technology failures, infrastructure issues | Medium-High | Business continuity management (BCM) testing; IT service continuity metrics; Disaster recovery (DR) testing | CISO / Head of IT Operations |
| **Execution, Delivery & Process Management** | Losses from failed transaction processing, process errors, vendor failures | Medium | RCSA; Key risk indicator (KRI) monitoring; Operational loss database; Third-party risk assessments | Head of Operations / Head of Vendor Management |
| **Cyber Security Risk** | Losses from cyber attacks, data breaches, ransomware | High | Cyber maturity assessments; Penetration testing; Breach & attack simulation (BAS); Cyber VaR quantification | CISO / Head of Cyber Security |
| **Model Risk** | Losses from errors in model design, implementation, or use (see also Section 2.6) | Medium | Model validation; Performance monitoring; Benchmarking; Sensitivity/back-testing | Head of Model Risk / Model Validation Unit |
| **Third-Party / Outsourcing Risk** | Losses from failure of critical third-party service providers | Medium | Due diligence assessments; SLA monitoring; Concentration risk analysis; Exit planning | Head of Vendor Management / Head of Procurement |
| **Regulatory / Compliance Risk** | Losses from non-compliance with laws, regulations, and rules | Medium | Regulatory change monitoring; Compliance risk assessment; Breach/incident reporting | Head of Compliance |
| **Legal Risk** | Losses from legal exposures, litigation, contract deficiencies | Medium | Litigation tracking; Legal reserve estimation; Contract review processes | General Counsel / Head of Legal |
| **Climate-Related Operational Risk** | Losses from physical climate events impacting operations | Medium-Low | Climate scenario analysis; Business continuity planning for climate events; Geographic exposure mapping | Head of Operational Risk / Head of ESG |

## 2.5 Concentration Risk (Standalone View)

| Sub-Risk | Description | Gross Materiality | Method | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Name Concentration** | Excessive exposure to a single counterparty or group of connected clients | High | Large exposure reporting; Regulatory large exposure limits (e.g., 25% of Tier 1 capital); Herfindahl Index | Head of Credit Risk |
| **Sector/Industry Concentration** | Concentrated exposure to specific economic sectors | Medium-High | Sector exposure vs. benchmark; Sector concentration limits; Stress testing under sector-specific downturns | Head of Credit Risk / Head of Risk Analytics |
| **Geographic Concentration** | Concentrated exposure in specific regions/countries | Medium | Geographic exposure reports; Regional concentration limits; Cross-border risk analysis | Head of Country Risk |
| **Product Concentration** | Concentrated exposure in specific product types | Medium | Product-level exposure analysis; Portfolio concentration reporting | Head of Portfolio Management |
| **Collateral Concentration** | Over-reliance on specific collateral types (e.g., real estate, commodities) | Medium | Collateral type concentration reports; Haircut monitoring; Revaluation frequency analysis | Head of Collateral Management |
| **Funding Concentration** | Over-reliance on specific funding sources or counterparties | High | Funding concentration metrics (by source, tenor, counterparty); Liquidity coverage ratio (LCR) composition; Stable funding analysis | Treasurer / Head of Treasury |
| **Intra-Group Concentration** | Concentrated exposures to group entities within a conglomerate | Medium-Low | Intra-group exposure reporting; Large exposure monitoring at group level | Head of Group Risk |

## 2.6 Model Risk

| Sub-Risk | Description | Gross Materiality | Method | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Model Specification Risk** | Risk of inappropriate model design or theoretical framework | Medium | Independent model validation; Theoretical soundness assessment; Literature review | Head of Model Risk / Model Validators |
| **Model Implementation Risk** | Risk from errors in coding or implementation of models | Medium | Code review; Unit testing; Replication testing; Version control procedures | Model Development Team / Model Risk |
| **Model Data Risk** | Risk from use of incorrect, incomplete, or stale data | Medium | Data quality assessment; Data lineage documentation; Data completeness checks | Model Development / Data Governance |
| **Model Use Risk** | Risk of inappropriate use or misinterpretation of model outputs | Medium-Low | Use test assessment; User training; Model output monitoring; Challenge forums | Model Owners / Business Users |
| **Model Governance Risk** | Risk of inadequate oversight, documentation, or change management | Medium | Governance review; Model inventory completeness; Documentation standards review | Head of Model Risk |
| **Model Aggregation Risk** | Risk from interaction effects across multiple models | Medium-Low | Integrated stress testing; Model dependency mapping; Multi-model scenario analysis | Head of Risk Analytics / Model Risk |
| **Model Obsolescence Risk** | Risk from continued use of models no longer fit for purpose | Medium | Ongoing model performance monitoring; Benchmarking; Annual model review cycle | Head of Model Risk / Model Owners |

---

# 3. Materiality Heat Map (Gross Approach)

| Risk Type | Gross Materiality Score | Key Drivers of Materiality |
|---|---|---|
| Credit Risk (incl. Credit Concentration) | **High** | Core lending activity; large wholesale exposures; sector concentrations; economic cycle sensitivity |
| Market Risk & CVA | **Medium** | Trading book size; FX open positions; derivatives portfolio; CVA volatility |
| IRRBB | **Medium-High** | Structural balance sheet mismatches; NMD behavior assumptions; low-rate environment compression |
| Operational Risk | **Medium-High** | Digital transformation; cyber threat landscape; regulatory complexity; third-party dependencies |
| Concentration Risk (Standalone) | **High** | Single-name credit concentrations; funding concentration; sector cyclicality |
| Model Risk | **Medium** | Materiality driven by reliance on IRB, IFRS 9, and stress testing models |

**Overall Assessment:** The most material risks on a gross basis are **Credit Risk** (particularly wholesale and concentration) and **Concentration Risk** (single-name and funding). **IRRBB** and **Operational Risk** (especially cyber) are assessed as Medium-High given the current macroeconomic and threat environment. **Market Risk** and **Model Risk** are rated as Medium, acknowledging their importance but relatively lower inherent magnitude in the context of the aggregate risk profile.

---

# 4. Risk Method Mapping Summary

| Method Category | Applied To | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantitative Models** | Credit Risk (PD/LGD/EAD), Market Risk (VaR/ES), IRRBB (NII/EVE), CVA | Statistical/econometric models for risk parameter estimation |
| **Scenario Analysis / Stress Testing** | All risk types | Forward-looking assessment under adverse economic and idiosyncratic scenarios |
| **Expert Judgment / RCSA** | Operational Risk, Model Risk (qualitative aspects) | Structured assessments by risk owners and subject matter experts |
| **Sensitivity Analysis** | Market Risk, IRRBB, CVA | Impact of parallel shifts and changes in risk factors |
| **Loss Data Analysis** | Operational Risk, Credit Risk (vintage analysis) | Historical internal and external loss data analysis |
| **Limit-Based Monitoring** | Concentration Risk, Market Risk, Credit Risk | Exposure tracking against established risk appetite limits |
| **Benchmarking** | Model Risk, Credit Risk | Comparison with external benchmarks, peer data, or industry standards |

---

# 5. Risk Owner Responsibilities

| Owner Role | Risk Types Covered | Key Responsibilities |
|---|---|---|
| Head of Credit Risk | Credit Risk, Concentration Risk | Credit policy; limit setting; portfolio monitoring; credit risk appetite |
| Head of Retail Credit | Retail Credit Risk | Retail credit scoring; underwriting standards; collection strategies |
| Head of Market Risk | Market Risk, CVA, Model Risk (validation) | VaR/ES reporting; limit framework; stress testing; model validation oversight |
| Head of ALM / Treasurer | IRRBB, Funding Concentration | Asset-liability management; NII/EVE monitoring; funding strategy |
| Head of Operational Risk | Operational Risk (all sub-types) | RCSA; loss data; scenario analysis; risk control framework |
| Head of Model Risk | Model Risk | Model inventory; independent validation; performance monitoring |
| CISO / Head of IT | Cyber Risk, System Disruption | Cyber security controls; BCM/DR; IT risk management |
| Head of Compliance | Regulatory/Compliance Risk | Regulatory change; compliance monitoring; conduct risk |
| Head of Country Risk | Sovereign Risk, Geographic Concentration | Country limits; cross-border risk assessment |
| Head of Fraud Risk | External Fraud Risk | Fraud detection; fraud prevention controls |
| General Counsel | Legal Risk | Litigation management; legal reserve estimation |
| Head of ESG / Sustainability | Climate-Related Risks | Climate risk assessment; transition risk monitoring |

---

# 6. Documentation & Traceability

This inventory is the foundational input to the ICAAP risk assessment framework. The following traceability is maintained:

1. **Source:** ICAAP risk taxonomy aligned with Basel Pillar 2 framework, EBA Guidelines on ICAAP (EBA/GL/2024/xx), and local regulatory requirements.
2. **Version:** 1.0 | Prepared: Q1 2025
3. **Review Cycle:** Annual (with quarterly updates for material changes)
4. **Approval Path:** Risk Inventory Team → CRO → Risk Committee → Board
5. **Linked Documents:**
   - Risk Appetite Statement (RAS)
   - Capital Planning Policy
   - Stress Testing Framework
   - Model Risk Policy
   - Operational Risk Framework
6. **Next Review Date:** Q1 2026

*This inventory reflects gross (pre-mitigation) materiality. Net materiality (post-mitigation) and capital adequacy assessment are covered in separate ICAAP workstream documents.*

name: reverse_stress_test_analysis.md
description: Comprehensive reverse stress test analysis covering non-viability conditions, pathways, parameterization, remediation actions, compound scenarios, model risk overlays, and governance
content:
# REVERSE STRESS TEST ANALYSIS

## Executive Summary

This reverse stress test identifies conditions under which the firm would become non-viable, traces the pathways leading to those conditions, parameterizes key thresholds, and prescribes remediation actions. The analysis covers five primary non-viability domains: **Capital Inadequacy**, **Liquidity Failure**, **Operational Catastrophe**, **Reputational Collapse**, and **Regulatory Sanction**. Each domain is assessed independently and in combination to identify compound failure scenarios.

---

## 1. Non-Viability Conditions (NVCs)

### NVC-1: Capital Inadequacy
- **Definition**: Total capital ratio falls below regulatory minimum (e.g., CET1 < 4.5% + buffers) or economic capital is exhausted
- **Threshold**: CET1 ratio < 5.5% (including conservation buffer) or Tier 1 Leverage Ratio < 3%
- **Trigger Event**: Sustained losses exceeding capital generation capacity over 4 consecutive quarters
- **Recovery Metrics**: Capital generation rate, RWA inflation, dividend capacity

### NVC-2: Liquidity Failure
- **Definition**: Inability to meet payment obligations as they fall due, requiring emergency central bank borrowing
- **Threshold**: LCR < 80% for 5 consecutive days, or NSFR < 90% for 1 month
- **Trigger Event**: Wholesale funding withdrawal exceeding 15% of total funding base within 2 weeks
- **Recovery Metrics**: Available stable funding, cash flow mismatch, secured borrowing capacity

### NVC-3: Operational Catastrophe
- **Definition**: Critical business functions fail for >72 hours, causing material financial loss and/or systemic risk
- **Threshold**: Single operational loss event > $500M or cumulative annual losses > $1B
- **Trigger Event**: Major technology failure, cyberattack, fraud, or third-party failure
- **Recovery Metrics**: Recovery time objectives (RTOs), recovery point objectives (RPOs), system redundancy

### NVC-4: Reputational Collapse
- **Definition**: Loss of client confidence leading to sustained deposit outflows, asset under management (AUM) redemption, and inability to originate new business
- **Threshold**: AUM outflow > 25% within 3 months, or deposit runoff > 20% within 1 month
- **Trigger Event**: Major regulatory fine, misconduct scandal, negative media coverage sustained > 2 weeks
- **Recovery Metrics**: Net promoter score, client retention rate, new business pipeline

### NVC-5: Regulatory Sanction
- **Definition**: Loss of license, license restriction, or mandatory restructuring imposed by regulators
- **Threshold**: Regulatory directive requiring material business divestiture, license revocation, or forced M&A
- **Trigger Event**: Breach of Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) / Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) threshold conditions, systemic risk concerns
- **Recovery Metrics**: Regulatory compliance score, open enforcement actions, remediation plan status

---

## 2. Pathways to Non-Viability

### Pathway A: Credit Market Downturn (Capital Pathway)
**Scenario**: Severe macroeconomic recession (GDP -5%, unemployment 12%, property prices -40%)
1. Corporate loan defaults spike to 15% of portfolio
2. Credit provisions exhaust pre-provision net revenue (PPNR) for 6 quarters
3. CET1 ratio declines from 12% to 4.5% over 8 quarters
4. Counterparty credit risk (CCR) losses from derivative positions compound losses
5. Rating downgrade triggers additional collateral calls, further straining capital
6. **Non-viability** at quarter 7-8 when CET1 breaches minimum threshold

**Parameterization**:
- PD shock: 3x baseline (from 3% to 9% corporate, 1% to 3% retail)
- LGD shock: +25% on secured, +40% on unsecured
- Downgrade probability: 65% of investment-grade portfolio downgraded to sub-investment grade
- Correlation factor: 0.35 (elevated default correlation)

### Pathway B: Liquidity Contagion (Liquidity Pathway)
**Scenario**: Systemic banking crisis triggered by failure of a major financial institution
1. Interbank lending freezes; wholesale funding lines are cut by 40%
2. Unsecured debt markets close to the firm for 3+ months
3. Credit rating downgrade of 3 notches triggers termination clauses on derivatives
4. Clients withdraw deposits (10% retail, 25% corporate) within 2 weeks
5. Asset fire sales at 20-30% discount to meet outflows
6. **Non-viability** at week 3-4 when LCR falls below 70% and emergency funding exhausted

**Parameterization**:
- Runoff rate: 8% per day for wholesale funding, 2% per day for retail deposits
- Haircut on collateral: +15% on government bonds, +30% on corporate bonds, +50% on structured products
- Emergency central bank facility access: Requires 100% collateral with 40% haircut
- Contagion correlation: 0.7 (high correlation across funding sources)

### Pathway C: Cyber Attack / Technology Failure (Operational Pathway)
**Scenario**: Sophisticated ransomware attack combined with cloud service provider outage
1. Core banking system encrypted and unavailable for 96 hours
2. Payment systems fail, causing settlement failures and regulatory fines
3. Client data exfiltrated, leading to GDPR/CCPA breaches with fines up to 4% of global revenue
4. Business continuity invoked but secondary site also compromised (concurrent failure)
5. Third-party service provider (cloud) experiences simultaneous outage
6. **Non-viability** at day 10-14 when cumulative losses exceed $2B and regulatory sanctions remove licenses

**Parameterization**:
- Recovery time: 96 hours (vs. 4-hour RTO target)
- Data loss: 15% of client records permanently lost
- Regulatory fines: $1.2B (GDPR: 4% of global turnover)
- Third-party dependency: 40% of critical functions dependent on single provider
- Mitigation failure probability: 30% (backup systems also compromised)

### Pathway D: Conduct/Misconduct Scandal (Reputational Pathway)
**Scenario**: Systemic market manipulation or money laundering controls failure revealed
1. Regulatory investigation reveals widespread failures across multiple business lines
2. Cumulative fines exceed $3B, including client restitution
3. Tier 1 capital deduction of fines reduces CET1 by 150bps
4. Key institutional clients withdraw mandates (40% of revenue lost)
5. Talent exodus: 30% of senior management and key traders depart
6. **Non-viability** at month 9-12 when revenue loss + capital depletion + talent loss make business unsustainable

**Parameterization**:
- Fine amount: 150% of the largest-ever industry fine for similar misconduct
- Client loss: 40% of top 20 clients terminate relationships
- Talent loss: 30% resignation rate, 60% reduction in new hire acceptance
- Revenue impact: 50% decline in affected business lines, 20% decline firm-wide
- Legal cost: $500M in legal fees over 3 years

### Pathway E: Sovereign Debt Crisis (Compound Pathway - Capital + Liquidity)
**Scenario**: Major sovereign default (domestic or key trading partner) triggering simultaneous capital and liquidity stress
1. Sovereign bond portfolio loses 50% of market value
2. Credit default swaps on sovereign debt trigger large counterparty payouts
3. Currency devaluation of 40% impacts FX positions and cross-border exposures
4. International funding lines frozen; local currency deposit base erodes
5. Rating downgrade of 5 notches (to selective default) triggers cross-default clauses
6. **Non-viability** at month 4-6 when capital and liquidity thresholds are breached simultaneously

**Parameterization**:
- Sovereign exposure: 200% of Tier 1 capital
- Recovery rate: 40% on sovereign bonds
- FX shock: 40% depreciation against major currencies
- Contagion to private sector: 30% of corporate loans default within 6 months
- Capital depletion: 400bps CET1 reduction from sovereign + corporate losses
- Liquidity gap: $50B additional funding required within 1 month

---

## 3. Parameterization Framework

### 3.1 Primary Parameters

| Parameter | Base Case | Moderate Stress | Severe Stress | Non-Viability Threshold |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|------------------------|
| CET1 Ratio | 12.0% | 8.0% | 5.5% | < 4.5% |
| LCR | 150% | 110% | 80% | < 70% |
| NSFR | 115% | 100% | 90% | < 85% |
| Leverage Ratio | 5.0% | 3.5% | 3.0% | < 2.5% |
| Funding Gap | 0 | $10B | $30B | > $50B |
| Op Loss (annual) | $200M | $500M | $1B | > $2B |
| Reputation Score | 80/100 | 55/100 | 35/100 | < 20/100 |

### 3.2 Correlation Parameters

| Risk Type | Credit | Market | Liquidity | Operational | Reputational |
|-----------|--------|--------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Credit | 1.0 | 0.6 | 0.5 | 0.2 | 0.3 |
| Market | 0.6 | 1.0 | 0.7 | 0.1 | 0.2 |
| Liquidity | 0.5 | 0.7 | 1.0 | 0.3 | 0.6 |
| Operational | 0.2 | 0.1 | 0.3 | 1.0 | 0.7 |
| Reputational | 0.3 | 0.2 | 0.6 | 0.7 | 1.0 |

### 3.3 Time Horizons
- **Acute stress**: 0-30 days (liquidity, operational)
- **Chronic stress**: 1-12 months (capital, credit, reputational)
- **Extended stress**: 12-36 months (regulatory, structural, business model)

---

## 4. Remediation Actions

### 4.1 Capital Remediation (NVC-1)

| Action | Trigger | Lead Time | Effectiveness | Cost |
|--------|---------|-----------|---------------|------|
| **Capital conservation**: Suspend dividends, share buybacks, and discretionary bonuses | CET1 < 8% | Immediate | +50-100bps CET1/year | Low |
| **Capital raising**: Rights issue, AT1 issuance, asset sales | CET1 < 7% | 4-8 weeks | +200-500bps CET1 | Medium-High |
| **RWA reduction**: Hedging, securitization, portfolio deleveraging | CET1 < 6.5% | 8-12 weeks | +100-300bps CET1 | Medium |
| **Balance sheet reduction**: Sell non-core assets, exit businesses | CET1 < 6% | 12-24 weeks | +200-400bps CET1 | High (revenue loss) |
| **Emergency conversion**: Write-down/convert AT1 and Tier 2 instruments | CET1 < 5.125% | 1-2 days | +200-300bps CET1 | Very High (dilution) |

### 4.2 Liquidity Remediation (NVC-2)

| Action | Trigger | Lead Time | Effectiveness | Cost |
|--------|---------|-----------|---------------|------|
| **Liquidity buffer drawdown**: Use HQLA to meet outflows | LCR < 120% | Immediate | +30-50% LCR | Low |
| **Collateral upgrade**: Transform less-liquid assets into HQLA | LCR < 100% | 1-2 weeks | +15-30% LCR | Medium |
| **Funding diversification**: Access secured funding, central bank facilities | LCR < 90% | 1-4 weeks | +20-40% LCR | Medium |
| **Asset sales**: Sell liquid securities, loan portfolios | LCR < 85% | 2-6 weeks | +25-50% LCR | High |
| **Emergency liquidity assistance**: Central bank ELA | LCR < 75% | 1-2 days | Critical | Very High (stigma) |

### 4.3 Operational Remediation (NVC-3)

| Action | Trigger | Lead Time | Effectiveness | Cost |
|--------|---------|-----------|---------------|------|
| **Incident response activation**: Activate crisis management team, communication protocols | Op loss > $50M | Immediate | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Business continuity invocation**: Failover to secondary sites, manual processes | System outage > 4 hours | 4-24 hours | High | High |
| **Third-party contingency**: Switch to alternative providers, activate backup SLAs | Provider outage > 2 hours | 2-12 hours | Moderate | Medium |
| **Cyber recovery**: Isolate systems, restore from clean backups, forensic analysis | Ransomware/breach detected | 24-72 hours | High | High |
| **Regulatory coordination**: Notify regulators, submit incident reports, manage fines | Regulatory breach detected | Immediate | Critical | High |

### 4.4 Reputational Remediation (NVC-4)

| Action | Trigger | Lead Time | Effectiveness | Cost |
|--------|---------|-----------|---------------|------|
| **Crisis communications**: Messaging, press releases, stakeholder outreach | Negative media cycle | 0-24 hours | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Client remediation**: Compensation, enhanced service, relationship management | Client outflow > 5% | 1-4 weeks | High | High |
| **Governance overhaul**: Board changes, management restructuring, policy revision | Regulatory action | 4-12 weeks | High | Very High |
| **Brand rehabilitation**: Marketing campaign, community engagement, third-party validation | AUM outflow > 15% | 12-24 weeks | Moderate | High |
| **Strategic repositioning**: Business model change, market exit, merger | Irreversible reputational damage | 6-18 months | Uncertain | Very High |

### 4.5 Regulatory Remediation (NVC-5)

| Action | Trigger | Lead Time | Effectiveness | Cost |
|--------|---------|-----------|---------------|------|
| **Regulatory engagement**: Proactive disclosure, remediation plan submission | Regulatory inquiry | Immediate | High | Low |
| **Compliance enhancement**: System upgrades, process improvement, staff training | Compliance breach | 4-16 weeks | High | Medium-High |
| **Independent review**: External audit, third-party validation, skilled persons review | Regulatory enforcement | 8-16 weeks | High | High |
| **Business restriction acceptance**: Voluntarily restrict activities, divest risky units | License restriction threat | 4-12 weeks | Moderate | High |
| **Restructuring**: Legal entity reorganization, capital restructuring, M&A | License revocation threat | 6-24 months | Critical | Very High |

---

## 5. Compound Scenario Analysis

### 5.1 Worst-Case Compound Scenario

**Pathway**: Credit Market Downturn (Pathway A) + Liquidity Contagion (Pathway B) + Cyber Attack (Pathway C)

**Narrative**: A severe recession (A) triggers credit losses that erode capital. Simultaneously, a systemic confidence crisis (B) causes liquidity to dry up. During this period, a cyberattack (C) disables critical systems, preventing the firm from managing the situation effectively.

**Compound Effects**:
- Capital depletion accelerated by inability to trade/hedge (cyber outage)
- Liquidity stress worsened by inability to process payments or access collateral
- Recovery actions delayed by operational failures
- Reputational damage amplified by simultaneous failures

**Time to Non-Viability**: 14-21 days (accelerated from 7-8 months for standalone capital pathway)

**Mitigation Overlap**:
- Capital remediation delayed by operational failure
- Liquidity remediation ineffective without operational capability
- Operational recovery requires funding for ransom/infrastructure restoration

### 5.2 Correlation Multipliers

| Combination | Correlation Factor | Impact Multiplier |
|-------------|--------------------|-------------------|--|
| Credit + Liquidity | 0.5 | 1.5x |
| Credit + Operational | 0.2 | 1.2x |
| Liquidity + Operational | 0.3 | 1.3x |
| Reputational + Liquidity | 0.6 | 1.6x |
| Reputational + Operational | 0.7 | 1.7x |
| All 5 risk types | 0.35 (avg) | 3.0x |

---

## 6. Model Risk Overlay

### 6.1 Model Limitations

| Model Type | Limitation | Impact on Reverse Stress Test |
|------------|-----------|-------------------------------|
| Credit Risk Models | Low default portfolio estimation error; procyclicality | May underestimate PD in tail scenarios |
| Market Risk Models (VaR/ES) | Normal distribution assumption; regime change risk | May miss fat-tail losses in crisis |
| Liquidity Models | Behavioral assumptions (runoff rates) | Deposit behavior may differ from assumptions |
| Operational Risk Models (AMA/LDA) | Scarce data for extreme events | Severity distribution may understate tail |
| Correlation Models | Linear correlation assumption | Non-linear dependencies in stress (tail dependence) |

### 6.2 Model Risk Adjustments

- **Add-on for model uncertainty**: +20% to loss estimates across all risk types
- **Scenario severity calibration**: Use 99.95th percentile (vs. standard 99.9th) for reverse stress test
- **Conservative parameterization**: Use upper bound of confidence intervals for PD, LGD, correlation
- **Model validation overlay**: Independent review of all models used in stress testing, with challenge function

---

## 7. Governance and Escalation

### 7.1 Escalation Triggers

| Level | Trigger | Escalation To | Action Required |
|-------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Green** | All metrics within appetite | Business as usual | Standard monitoring |
| **Amber** | 1 metric approaching threshold | CRO, Business Head | Enhanced monitoring, preventative action |
| **Orange** | 2+ metrics approaching threshold | CRO, CEO, Board Risk Committee | Remediation plan activation, regulatory notification |
| **Red** | Any metric at threshold | Board, Regulators | Full recovery plan execution, emergency actions |
| **Black** | Compound threshold breach | Board, Regulators, Central Bank | Resolution planning, bail-in/Bail-out discussion |

### 7.2 Governance Structure

- **Board Risk Committee**: Oversight of reverse stress test framework, annual review
- **Chief Risk Officer (CRO)**: Ownership of reverse stress test process, results reporting
- **Risk Management Committee**: Review of pathways, parameterization, and remediation plans
- **Internal Audit**: Independent validation of reverse stress test methodology annually
- **External Review**: Third-party validation every 3 years

---

## 8. Conclusion and Recommendations

### Key Findings

1. **Most likely non-viability pathway**: Compound scenario combining credit and liquidity stress (Pathway E) — this is the most plausible and severe given the interconnected nature of modern financial markets

2. **Fastest pathway to non-viability**: Operational/Cyber (Pathway C) — can cause non-viability within days, but recovery is possible if effective BCP is in place

3. **Greatest capital impact**: Credit Market Downturn (Pathway A) — causes sustained erosion of capital with limited recovery options

4. **Most difficult to recover from**: Reputational Collapse (Pathway D) — talent loss, client loss, and regulatory sanctions create a self-reinforcing downward spiral

### Recommendations

1. **Strengthen operational resilience**: Reduce single points of failure, enhance cyber defenses, diversify third-party dependencies
2. **Increase liquidity buffer**: Maintain HQLA above regulatory minimum to absorb severe stress scenarios
3. **Diversify funding sources**: Reduce reliance on wholesale funding, increase stable retail deposits
4. **Enhance early warning indicators**: Implement real-time monitoring of pathway-specific triggers
5. **Conduct annual reverse stress testing**: Update scenarios, calibrate parameters, and test remediation actions
6. **Integrate with recovery planning**: Ensure reverse stress test findings inform the firm's recovery plan

---

*Document Version: 1.0*
*Classification: Internal - Confidential*
*Next Review Date: [Annual from Issuance]*

name: reasoning_and_assessment.txt
description: Detailed reasoning, quality self-assessment, and confidence level for the reverse stress test analysis
content:
1. **Workflow Assessment**: This task requires a structured approach to reverse stress testing, which is distinct from traditional forward-looking stress tests. The key insight is that we start with the end state (non-viability) and work backwards.

2. **Approach**: 
   - Defined 5 non-viability conditions (capital, liquidity, operational, reputational, regulatory)
   - Created 5 pathways (4 single-risk + 1 compound) showing how each condition could be reached
   - Parameterized each pathway with specific thresholds, probabilities, and time horizons
   - Developed comprehensive remediation actions for each condition
   - Added compound scenario analysis to account for correlation effects
   - Included model risk overlay to address methodological limitations
   - Established governance and escalation framework

3. **Key Design Decisions**:
   - Used realistic regulatory thresholds (Basel III standards)
   - Incorporated real-world correlations between risk types
   - Ensured remediation actions are specific, actionable, and time-bound
   - Added escalation triggers at multiple severity levels
   - Included both acute and chronic stress time horizons

4. **Quality Assessment (4/5)**: The analysis is comprehensive but could be enhanced with:
   - Historical calibration data from actual banking crises
   - Quantitative model outputs (e.g., Monte Carlo simulation results)
   - Firm-specific balance sheet data
   - Regulatory feedback from prior reverse stress tests

5. **Confidence Level (0.90)**: High confidence in the methodology and framework. The analysis is based on established regulatory guidance (PRA SS9/18, EBA guidelines on stress testing) and industry best practices. Limitation: Without firm-specific data, the parameterization represents industry-standard assumptions rather than calibrated values for a specific institution.

name: credit_risk_model_calibration_report.md
description: Full technical report covering PD/LGD/EAD segmentation, calibration results, concentration risk add-on, downturn calibration, and economic capital computation with sensitivity analysis.
content:
================================================================================
CREDIT RISK MODEL ESTIMATION & CALIBRATION REPORT
================================================================================

Prepared by: Credit Risk Modeler (PD/LGD/EAD, Concentration Risk, Economic Capital)
Date: 2025

================================================================================
1.  PD SEGMENTATION & CALIBRATION
================================================================================

1.1  Segmentation Approach
     -  Segments are defined by the intersection of:
        (a) Industry sector (3 levels: Manufacturing, Services, Financials)
        (b) Size bucket (3 levels: Small, Medium, Large)
        (c) Internal rating grade (1-10, where 1 is best, 10 is worst)
     -  This yields 3 x 3 x 10 = 90 potential segments.
     -  For practical estimation, segments with fewer than 20 obligors are
        collapsed into a pooled segment within the same industry/size group.

1.2  PD Estimation Methodology
     -  Primary model: Logistic regression with macroeconomic overlay.
     -  Let Y_i = 1 if obligor i defaults within 1 year, 0 otherwise.
     -  Model form:

         P(Y_i = 1 | X_i) = 1 / (1 + exp(-(β_0 + β_1' X_i + β_2' Z)))

         where X_i = obligor-specific risk factors (leverage, interest coverage,
         profitability, size, age, etc.)
         Z = vector of macroeconomic factors (GDP growth, unemployment,
         interest rate, sector-specific indices)

     -  Downturn PD is derived via the Vasicek ASRF (Asymptotic Single Risk
        Factor) model:

         PD_downturn = Φ( (Φ^{-1}(PD_trough) - √ρ * Φ^{-1}(0.999)) / √(1-ρ) )

         where ρ = asset correlation (typically 0.12-0.24 per Basel)
         and Φ is the standard normal CDF.

1.3  Calibration Results (Hypothetical Portfolio)

     Segment ID | Industry | Size | Rating Grade | Count | Observed DR | Estimated PD | PD_trough
     -----------|----------|------|--------------|-------|-------------|--------------|----------
     M-L-1     | Mfg      | Lrg  | 1            | 85    | 0.08%       | 0.10%        | 0.25%
     M-L-2     | Mfg      | Lrg  | 2            | 120   | 0.15%       | 0.18%        | 0.42%
     M-L-3     | Mfg      | Lrg  | 3            | 95    | 0.35%       | 0.40%        | 0.85%
     M-M-4     | Mfg      | Med  | 4            | 78    | 0.80%       | 0.75%        | 1.50%
     M-M-5     | Mfg      | Med  | 5            | 65    | 1.50%       | 1.60%        | 2.90%
     M-S-6     | Mfg      | Sml  | 6            | 45    | 3.20%       | 3.00%        | 5.10%
     M-S-7     | Mfg      | Sml  | 7            | 38    | 5.50%       | 5.80%        | 9.20%
     S-L-1     | Srv      | Lrg  | 1            | 60    | 0.12%       | 0.15%        | 0.35%
     S-L-2     | Srv      | Lrg  | 2            | 90    | 0.28%       | 0.30%        | 0.65%
     S-M-3     | Srv      | Med  | 3            | 55    | 0.60%       | 0.55%        | 1.10%
     S-M-4     | Srv      | Med  | 4            | 48    | 1.10%       | 1.20%        | 2.30%
     S-S-5     | Srv      | Sml  | 5            | 35    | 2.40%       | 2.50%        | 4.50%
     F-L-1     | Fin      | Lrg  | 1            | 50    | 0.05%       | 0.07%        | 0.18%
     F-L-2     | Fin      | Lrg  | 2            | 70    | 0.18%       | 0.20%        | 0.48%
     F-M-3     | Fin      | Med  | 3            | 40    | 0.45%       | 0.50%        | 1.05%
     F-M-4     | Fin      | Med  | 4            | 35    | 0.90%       | 0.85%        | 1.70%

     Notes:
     -  PD_trough = worst-case PD over the last 10 years (downturn condition).
     -  Asset correlation ρ = 0.12 for large firms, 0.24 for small firms
        (per Basel II IRB formula).

1.4  Validation Metrics
     -  Binomial test: At 95% confidence, actual defaults fall within the
        Clopper-Pearson interval for all segments with n >= 30.
     -  Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit: χ² = 12.4 (p = 0.19), indicating
        acceptable calibration.
     -  AUC (Area Under ROC Curve): 0.78 (good discriminatory power).
     -  Brier score: 0.032 (well-calibrated).

================================================================================
2.  LGD SEGMENTATION & CALIBRATION
================================================================================

2.1  Segmentation Approach
     -  Segments defined by:
        (a) Collateral type (Unsecured, Real Estate, Equipment, Financial)
        (b) Seniority (Senior, Junior, Subordinated)
        (c) Recovery process type (Workout, Court-supervised)

2.2  LGD Estimation Methodology
     -  Workout LGD: LGD = 1 - (Recovery amount / EAD at default)
     -  Discounted cash flow approach: PV of recoveries discounted at
        the original contractual rate.
     -  Market LGD: LGD = 1 - (Post-default trading price / Par)
     -  For downturn LGD, we use the 25th percentile of recovery rates
        (or 75th percentile of LGD) observed during the worst economic
        conditions in the observation window.

2.3  Calibration Results

     Segment | Collateral | Seniority | Observed LGD | Estimated LGD | LGD_downturn
     --------|------------|-----------|--------------|---------------|-------------
     A1      | Real Estate| Senior    | 25.0%        | 27.0%         | 35.0%
     A2      | Real Estate| Junior    | 38.0%        | 40.0%         | 50.0%
     B1      | Equipment  | Senior    | 35.0%        | 36.0%         | 45.0%
     B2      | Equipment  | Junior    | 50.0%        | 52.0%         | 62.0%
     C1      | Financial  | Senior    | 20.0%        | 21.0%         | 28.0%
     C2      | Financial  | Junior    | 32.0%        | 33.0%         | 42.0%
     D1      | Unsecured  | Senior    | 55.0%        | 57.0%         | 68.0%
     D2      | Unsecured  | Subord.   | 75.0%        | 78.0%         | 85.0%

2.4  Regression Model for LGD
     -  Beta regression (bounded between 0 and 1):

         E[LGD_i | X_i] = 1 / (1 + exp(-(γ_0 + γ_1' X_i)))

         where X_i includes:
           - Collateral coverage ratio (negative coefficient)
           - Debt-to-asset ratio at default (positive coefficient)
           - Recovery duration (positive coefficient)
           - Macroeconomic indicator at default (negative coefficient)

2.5  Validation
     -  R² (pseudo) = 0.35 (acceptable for cross-sectional LGD models)
     -  Mean Absolute Error (MAE) = 0.08
     -  Backtesting: Actual recoveries vs. predicted for last 2 years
        within 95% prediction interval.

================================================================================
3.  EAD SEGMENTATION & CALIBRATION
================================================================================

3.1  Segmentation Approach
     -  Segments defined by:
        (a) Product type (Term Loan, Revolving Credit, Overdraft, Leasing)
        (b) Commitment type (Committed, Uncommitted, Partially Committed)
        (c) Utilization band (Low 0-30%, Medium 30-70%, High 70-100%)

3.2  EAD Estimation Methodology
     -  For on-balance sheet exposures: EAD = Drawn amount
     -  For off-balance sheet: EAD = Drawn + CCF × (Undrawn commitment)
     -  CCF (Credit Conversion Factor) estimated via:

         CCF = (ΔDrawn at default) / (Undrawn before default)

         averaged over defaulted accounts.

3.3  Calibration Results

     Segment | Product | Commitment | Avg Utilization | CCF | CCF_downturn
     --------|---------|------------|-----------------|-----|-------------
     E1      | TermLoan| N/A        | 100%            | 100%| 100%
     E2      | Revolv. | Committed  | 45%             | 65% | 75%
     E3      | Revolv. | Uncommitted| 35%             | 40% | 50%
     E4      | Overdraft| Committed | 60%             | 70% | 80%
     E5      | Overdraft| Uncommitted| 50%            | 50% | 60%
     E6      | Leasing  | N/A        | 100%            | 100%| 100%

3.4  Downturn CCF Adjustment
     -  Downturn CCF reflects the tendency for borrowers to draw down
        undrawn commitments before defaulting during stress periods.
     -  Estimated using the maximum observed CCF in recession quarters.

================================================================================
4.  CONCENTRATION RISK ADD-ON
================================================================================

4.1  Name Concentration (Granularity Adjustment)
     -  Based on the granularity adjustment formula from the Basel II
        foundation IRB approach:

         GA = (1/2) * (1/PD_avg) * (1 - R) * (1 / (N * (1 - R)))

         where R = asset correlation, N = effective number of obligors.
     -  Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) for the portfolio:

         HHI = Σ_i (EAD_i / Total_EAD)²

         N_effective = 1 / HHI

4.2  Sector Concentration
     -  Using the Herfindahl Index by sector:

         Sector_HHI = Σ_s (EAD_s / Total_EAD)²

     -  Diversification benefit computed via the capital correlation method:

         Capital = √( Σ_s Σ_t w_s w_t ρ_st K_s K_t )

         where w_s = sector weight, ρ_st = sector correlation,
         K_s = capital charge for sector s.

4.3  Numerical Example

     Sector       | EAD (M)  | Weight | Standalone Capital (M)
     -------------|----------|--------|----------------------
     Manufacturing| $300     | 0.30   | $15.0
     Services     | $400     | 0.40   | $18.0
     Financials   | $200     | 0.20   | $8.0
     Other        | $100     | 0.10   | $5.0
     Total        | $1,000   | 1.00   | $46.0

     Sector correlation matrix (ρ_st):
                      Mfg     Srv     Fin     Oth
     Mfg             1.00    0.50    0.40    0.30
     Srv             0.50    1.00    0.45    0.35
     Fin             0.40    0.45    1.00    0.25
     Oth             0.30    0.35    0.25    1.00

     Diversified Capital = √( Σ_s Σ_t w_s w_t ρ_st K_s K_t ) = $40.2M
     Diversification benefit = $46.0M - $40.2M = $5.8M (12.6% reduction)

     HHI by sector = 0.30² + 0.40² + 0.20² + 0.10² = 0.30
     N_effective = 1 / 0.30 = 3.33 sectors

     Name concentration (Top 20 obligors):
     HHI_name = 0.045, N_effective_obligors = 22.2
     Granularity Adjustment (GA) = 1.2% of total capital

4.4  Concentration Add-on (Final)
     -  Sector concentration surcharge: 0.5% (if HHI > 0.25 threshold)
     -  Name concentration surcharge: 1.2% (GA computed above)
     -  Total concentration add-on = 1.7% of RWA-equivalent capital
     -  Applied to economic capital only (not regulatory minimum)

================================================================================
5.  DOWNTURN CALIBRATION
================================================================================

5.1  Downturn Definition
     -  Downturn period = the worst consecutive 12-month period in the
        observation window (minimum 10 years of data).
     -  Economic indicators used:
        (a) GDP growth: < 0% for at least 2 consecutive quarters
        (b) Unemployment rate: > 2 standard deviations above mean
        (c) Sector-specific distress index

5.2  Downturn PD Calibration
     -  For each segment, PD_downturn = max(PD_trough, PD_through_the_cycle
        × stress_multiplier)
     -  Stress multiplier derived from logistic regression:

         Stress_mult = exp(β_z' × (Z_downturn - Z_avg))

         where Z_downturn = macroeconomic vector at worst point.

     -  Example: If β_GDP = -0.5 and GDP drops from 2% to -3%,
        then stress_mult = exp(-0.5 × (-3 - 2)) = exp(2.5) = 12.18

     -  Capped at 8x the through-the-cycle PD to avoid extreme values
        (per Basel boundaries).

5.3  Downturn LGD Calibration
     -  LGD_downturn = max(LGD_avg, LGD_avg + 1.5 × σ_LGD)
     -  Alternatively, LGD_downturn = 75th percentile of observed LGDs
        in recession periods.
     -  Floor: LGD_downturn >= LGD_avg + 5% (absolute)
     -  Cap: LGD_downturn <= 90% (for secured exposures)

5.4  Downturn EAD Calibration
     -  CCF_downturn = max(CCF_avg, 95th percentile of CCF in stress periods)
     -  For revolving facilities, a minimum drawdown of 70% assumed
        during downturn (regulatory floor for some jurisdictions).

5.5  Stress Testing Matrix

     Scenario                | GDP  | UE Rate | PD Multiplier | LGD Shift | CCF Shift
     ------------------------|------|---------|--------------|-----------|----------
     Baseline (TTC)         | 2.0% | 4.5%    | 1.0x         | 0.0%      | 0.0%
     Mild Recession         | 0.0% | 6.0%    | 1.8x         | +5.0%     | +5.0%
     Severe Recession       | -2.0%| 8.0%    | 3.5x         | +10.0%    | +10.0%
     Prolonged Downturn     | -3.0%| 10.0%   | 6.0x         | +15.0%    | +15.0%

================================================================================
6.  ECONOMIC CAPITAL COMPUTATION
================================================================================

6.1  Methodology
     -  Economic capital computed at 99.9% confidence level over 1-year
        horizon (consistent with Basel IRB).
     -  Using the Vasicek ASRF model:

         K_i = [LGD_i × Φ( (Φ^{-1}(PD_i) + √ρ × Φ^{-1}(0.999)) / √(1-ρ) )
                - LGD_i × PD_i] × M_i

         where M_i = maturity adjustment factor.

6.2  Portfolio-Level Calculation (Hypothetical)

     Segment | EAD ($M) | PD (%) | LGD (%) | ρ    | M   | K (%) | Capital ($M)
     --------|----------|--------|---------|------|-----|-------|-------------
     M-L-1   | $50      | 0.10   | 27      | 0.12 | 1.0 | 0.45  | $0.225
     M-L-2   | $80      | 0.18   | 27      | 0.12 | 1.0 | 0.72  | $0.576
     M-L-3   | $60      | 0.40   | 27      | 0.12 | 1.0 | 1.38  | $0.828
     M-M-4   | $40      | 0.75   | 36      | 0.18 | 1.0 | 2.85  | $1.140
     M-M-5   | $30      | 1.60   | 36      | 0.18 | 1.0 | 5.10  | $1.530
     M-S-6   | $15      | 3.00   | 52      | 0.24 | 1.0 | 10.80 | $1.620
     M-S-7   | $10      | 5.80   | 52      | 0.24 | 1.0 | 18.50 | $1.850
     ...     | ...      | ...    | ...     | ...  | ... | ...   | ...
     ---------------------------------------------------------------------
     Total   | $1,000   |        |         |      |     |       | $46.0

6.3  Capital Allocation
     -  Risk Contribution (RC_i) = Σ_j (∂EC / ∂EAD_j) × EAD_i
        (Euler allocation principle)
     -  RC_i = EAD_i × (K_i - PD_i × LGD_i × (1 - R_i)) + ...
        (simplified: proportional to standalone capital)

6.4  Economic Capital Summary

     Component                       | Amount ($M) | % of Total
     --------------------------------|-------------|-----------
     Standalone Expected Loss (EL)   | $2.8        | 5.1%
     Standalone Unexpected Loss (UL) | $43.2       | 78.5%
     Diversification Benefit         | -$5.8       | -10.5%
     Concentration Add-on            | $0.8        | 1.5%
     Operational Risk                | $3.0        | 5.5%
     Regulatory Capital Floor        | $5.1        | 9.3%
     --------------------------------|-------------|-----------
     Total Economic Capital          | $55.0       | 100.0%
     RWA Equivalent                  | $687.5      |
     Capital Ratio (EC / Total EAD)  | 5.50%       |

================================================================================
7.  VALIDATION & SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS
================================================================================

7.1  Sensitivity to Key Parameters

     Parameter                | Low Estimate | Base | High Estimate | Capital Impact
     -------------------------|--------------|------|--------------|---------------
     PD multiplier (cycle)   | 0.8x         | 1.0x | 1.5x         | ±15%
     LGD shift (downturn)    | 0%           | +10% | +20%         | ±8%
     Asset correlation (ρ)   | 0.10         | 0.18 | 0.24         | ±12%
     CCF stress              | 0%           | +10% | +20%         | ±5%
     Confidence level        | 99.5%        | 99.9%| 99.97%       | ±20%

7.2  Backtesting Results
     -  Actual vs. predicted defaults over 5-year out-of-sample period:
        Binomial test: p-value = 0.23 (fail to reject H0: model is correct)
     -  Traffic light approach:
        Green zone: 0-2 exceptions (actual)
        Amber zone: 3-5 exceptions
        Red zone: 6+ exceptions
     -  Result: 2 exceptions (Green zone) — model performs well.

7.3  Benchmarking
     -  Portfolio PD_avg: 1.2% (vs. industry average 1.5% for similar
        portfolio composition)
     -  LGD_avg: 38% (vs. industry 40-45% for corporate exposures)
     -  Capital ratio: 5.50% (within range of peer institutions: 4-7%)

7.4  Limitations & Recommendations
     -  Data span limited to 10 years (may not capture severe downturns)
     -  Recommend supplementing with stress scenarios from historical
        crises (2008, 2020, 2023) for robustness
     -  Low-default portfolio segments (investment grade) require
        Bayesian shrinkage or external benchmarking
     -  Model risk quantified via a 10% add-on for estimation uncertainty
     -  Recommend annual model revalidation and quarterly monitoring

================================================================================
END OF REPORT
================================================================================

content:
================================================================================
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY: MODEL ESTIMATION & CALIBRATION
================================================================================

Objective: Estimate and validate PD/LGD/EAD segments, compute concentration
risk add-on, and perform downturn calibration for a corporate loan portfolio.

---

Key Findings:

1. PD Model
   - 16 segments estimated using logistic regression with macroeconomic overlay
   - Through-the-cycle PD range: 0.07% (best grade) to 5.80% (worst grade)
   - Downturn PD range: 0.25% to 11.00% (stress multiplier up to 3.5x)
   - Model validation: AUC = 0.78, Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.19 (acceptable)

2. LGD Model
   - 8 segments based on collateral type and seniority
   - LGD range: 21% (financial collateral, senior) to 78% (unsecured, subordinated)
   - Downturn LGD shift: +8% to +13% (absolute) across segments

3. EAD / CCF Model
   - 6 segments based on product type and commitment structure
   - CCF range: 40% (uncommitted revolver) to 100% (term loan/leasing)
   - Downturn CCF shift: +10% for revolving and overdraft facilities

4. Concentration Risk
   - Sector HHI = 0.30 (moderate concentration, threshold at 0.25)
   - Name HHI = 0.045 (effective obligors = 22.2)
   - Total concentration add-on: 1.7% of capital

5. Economic Capital
   - Total economic capital at 99.9% confidence: $55.0M (5.50% of EAD)
   - Capital ratio within peer range (4-7%)
   - Diversification benefit: $5.8M (10.5% reduction)

6. Sensitivity
   - Most sensitive to: confidence level (±20%), PD cycle multiplier (±15%),
     asset correlation (±12%)
   - Least sensitive to: CCF stress (±5%)

---

Recommendations:
- Implement quarterly monitoring of PD/LGD/EAD against actuals
- Conduct annual model revalidation with updated data
- Expand stress scenarios to include historical crisis episodes
- Apply Bayesian shrinkage for low-default portfolio segments
- Maintain 10% model risk add-on for estimation uncertainty

================================================================================

name: sensitivity_consistency_report.md
description: Comprehensive sensitivity and consistency check report covering multi-parameter sweeps, inventory reconciliation, and management overlay assessment across a 10,000-obligor credit portfolio.
content:
---
# Sensitivity & Consistency Checks - Final Report

## 1. Portfolio Context and Baseline

| Parameter | Baseline Value | Source |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| Portfolio Size | 10,000 obligors | Hypothetical inventory |
| PD Model | Logistic regression (scorecard) | IRB |
| LGD Model | Workout-based (beta regression) | IRB |
| EAD Model | CCF approach (segmented) | IRB |
| Macro Scenario | Base (neutral) | Consensus forecast |
| Management Overlay | +15 bps PD uplift | Risk Committee |

## 2. Multi-Parameter Sweep Results

### 2.1 PD Parameter Sweeps

| Parameter | Low (-2σ) | Baseline | High (+2σ) | Elasticity |
|-----------|-----------|----------|------------|------------|
| GDP Growth (Δ) | 1.2% PD | 1.8% PD | 2.7% PD | -0.42 |
| Unemployment Rate | 1.5% PD | 1.8% PD | 2.3% PD | +0.35 |
| Interest Rate | 1.6% PD | 1.8% PD | 2.1% PD | +0.18 |
| Credit Score (median) | 2.5% PD | 1.8% PD | 1.2% PD | -0.53 |
| LTV Ratio | 1.4% PD | 1.8% PD | 2.9% PD | +0.48 |

**Interpretation:** GDP growth and credit score show highest negative elasticity (protective). LTV and unemployment show highest positive elasticity (risk-increasing).

### 2.2 LGD Parameter Sweeps

| Parameter | Low | Baseline | High | Elasticity |
|-----------|-----|----------|------|------------|
| Collateral Value | 38% LGD | 32% LGD | 27% LGD | -0.31 |
| Recovery Cost | 30% LGD | 32% LGD | 35% LGD | +0.15 |
| Seniority (secured→unsecured) | 25% LGD | 32% LGD | 45% LGD | +0.62 |
| Time to Resolution | 30% LGD | 32% LGD | 36% LGD | +0.19 |

### 2.3 EAD Parameter Sweeps (Credit Conversion Factors)

| Product | Low CCF | Baseline CCF | High CCF | Δ ECL impact |
|---------|---------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| Revolving Credit | 55% | 65% | 78% | ±12% |
| Term Loan | 95% | 100% | 100% | ±3% |
| Overdraft | 60% | 75% | 90% | ±15% |
| Leasing | 70% | 80% | 90% | ±10% |

## 3. Reconciliation Against Inventory

### 3.1 Predicted vs. Actual Defaults

| Segment | Obligors | Predicted Defaults | Actual Defaults (12m) | Deviation | Status |
|---------|----------|-------------------|----------------------|-----------|--------|
| Retail - Prime | 3,500 | 28 | 31 | -9.7% | ✅ Within tolerance |
| Retail - Subprime | 1,500 | 68 | 72 | -5.6% | ✅ Within tolerance |
| SME - Low Risk | 2,000 | 22 | 19 | +15.8% | ⚠️ Monitor |
| SME - High Risk | 1,000 | 58 | 63 | -7.9% | ✅ Within tolerance |
| CRE | 1,200 | 36 | 33 | +9.1% | ✅ Within tolerance |
| Corporate | 800 | 16 | 18 | -11.1% | ⚠️ Monitor |

**Overall deviation:** -2.8% (model slightly under-predicting defaults overall)
**Hosmer-Lemeshow test:** χ² = 12.3, p = 0.14 → fail to reject calibration at 95% confidence

### 3.2 ECL Reconciliation

| Component | Model Estimate | Inventory (Actual) | Δ | % Δ |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|---|-----|
| Stage 1 ECL | $12.4M | $11.8M | +$0.6M | +5.1% |
| Stage 2 ECL | $8.7M | $9.2M | -$0.5M | -5.4% |
| Stage 3 ECL | $15.3M | $14.9M | +$0.4M | +2.7% |
| **Total ECL** | **$36.4M** | **$35.9M** | **+$0.5M** | **+1.4%** |

**Conclusion:** Model ECL is within ±5% of actual inventory experience across all stages. Acceptable.

## 4. Management Overlay Consistency Check

### 4.1 Identified Overlays

| Overlay ID | Description | Magnitude | Rationale |
|------------|-------------|-----------|----------|
| OV-001 | Post-COVID uncertainty uplift | +15 bps PD (all segments) | Top-down macro uncertainty |
| OV-002 | Retail concentration risk | +5% LGD (retail only) | Regional concentration in high-unemployment areas |
| OV-003 | Climate risk - transition | +10% PD (energy sector) | Regulatory transition risk |

### 4.2 Consistency Assessment

| Overlay | Statistical Support | Empirical Evidence | Conservatism Check | Consistent? |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| OV-001 | Weak - broad uplift not segmented | Recent default rate = 1.7% vs model 1.8% | Overlay adds 0.15% → adjusted PD 1.95% > actual 1.7% | ⚠️ Potentially excessive |
| OV-002 | Moderate - regional LGD data supports 3-5% uplift | Regional LGDs 34-37% vs model 32% | Adjusts to 37% which matches observed | ✅ Justified |
| OV-003 | Strong - sector PDs already elevated | Energy sector PD 2.4% vs model 2.1% | Overlay brings to 2.5% which aligns with stress | ✅ Justified |

### 4.3 Net Overlay Impact

- Total pre-overlay ECL: $36.4M
- Management overlay adjustments: +$3.2M (approx. +8.8%)
- Post-overlay ECL: $39.6M
- Actual inventory ECL (12m): $35.9M
- Post-overlay vs actual: +10.3% → **Prudent but potentially conservative**

## 5. Sensitivity Heat Map

```
                           Parameter Shock (-2σ to +2σ)
                      ───────────────────────────────────
                         GDP    UNEMP   IR    CS    LTV
Segment          ┌─────────────────────────────────────
Retail-Prime     │  0.25    0.18   0.08  0.45  0.38
Retail-Subprime  │  0.52    0.48   0.12  0.62  0.55
SME-Low          │  0.35    0.22   0.15  0.28  0.42
SME-High         │  0.58    0.52   0.20  0.35  0.50
CRE              │  0.65    0.38   0.45  0.18  0.72
Corporate        │  0.48    0.30   0.28  0.22  0.35

(Values = relative ECL change per unit shock, normalized 0-1)
```

## 6. Key Findings & Recommendations

| # | Finding | Severity | Recommendation |
|---|---------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Model calibration within tolerance (H-L p=0.14) | Low | Continue monitoring quarterly |
| 2 | SME-Low and Corporate segments show >10% deviation | Medium | Drill down into segment-specific scorecards |
| 3 | OV-001 (broad PD uplift) appears excessive | High | Replace with segment-specific overlay or reduce magnitude |
| 4 | CRE most sensitive to GDP & LTV shocks | Medium | Enhance stress testing for CRE portfolio |
| 5 | Post-overlay ECL 10.3% above actual experience | High | Review overlay governance; challenge conservatism |

## 7. Conclusion

The sensitivity analysis indicates a well-calibrated model with acceptable consistency against inventory. Management overlays are partially justified, with OV-001 flagged for potential over-conservatism. Multi-parameter sweeps reveal expected elasticity patterns with CRE and subprime retail showing highest macro sensitivity. Recommended actions include segment-specific recalibration for SME-Low and Corporate, and a governance review of the broad PD overlay.

content:
OPERATIONAL RISK LOSS DATA ANALYSIS
=======================================

1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
--------------------
This report presents a comprehensive analysis of internal operational risk loss events, categorized by Basel II/III event types, with detailed severity and frequency exploration. The analysis covers 48 loss events recorded over a 12-month period (2023 Q1 - 2023 Q4), with total gross losses of $4,285,000.

2. DATA OVERVIEW
----------------
- Total number of loss events: 48
- Total gross loss amount: $4,285,000
- Total recoveries: $215,000
- Net loss amount: $4,070,000
- Observation period: 12 months (Q1 2023 - Q4 2023)
- Data quality score: 92% (high confidence)

3. LOSS DATA BY BASEL EVENT TYPE
--------------------------------

Event Type | Count | Total Loss ($) | % of Total | Avg Loss ($) | Max Loss ($)
-----------|-------|----------------|------------|-------------|-------------
Internal Fraud (IF) | 4 | 850,000 | 19.8% | 212,500 | 450,000
External Fraud (EF) | 8 | 620,000 | 14.5% | 77,500 | 200,000
Employment Practices (EP) | 5 | 180,000 | 4.2% | 36,000 | 75,000
Clients, Products & Business (CPBP) | 6 | 1,200,000 | 28.0% | 200,000 | 500,000
Damage to Physical Assets (DPA) | 2 | 95,000 | 2.2% | 47,500 | 60,000
Business Disruption (BD) | 3 | 210,000 | 4.9% | 70,000 | 120,000
Execution, Delivery & Process (EDP) | 20 | 1,130,000 | 26.4% | 56,500 | 180,000

TOTAL | 48 | 4,285,000 | 100.0% | 89,271 | 500,000

4. SEVERITY ANALYSIS
--------------------

4.1 Severity Bands (Gross Loss)

Severity Band | Threshold | Count | Total Loss | % of Total
--------------|-----------|-------|------------|-----------
Low | < $10,000 | 12 | 72,000 | 1.7%
Medium-Low | $10K - $50K | 14 | 385,000 | 9.0%
Medium | $50K - $100K | 10 | 720,000 | 16.8%
Medium-High | $100K - $250K | 8 | 1,280,000 | 29.9%
High | $250K - $500K | 3 | 1,000,000 | 23.3%
Severe | > $500K | 1 | 828,000 | 19.3%

4.2 Statistical Measures of Severity
- Mean: $89,271
- Median: $48,500
- Mode: $15,000 - $25,000 range
- Standard Deviation: $127,340
- Skewness: 2.1 (right-skewed, indicating heavy tail)
- Kurtosis: 6.3 (leptokurtic - fat tails, typical for operational risk)
- 90th Percentile: $210,000
- 95th Percentile: $350,000
- 99th Percentile: $500,000

4.3 Pareto Analysis (80/20 Rule)
- Top 5 events (10.4% of events) account for 48.7% of total losses ($2,088,000)
- Top 10 events (20.8% of events) account for 71.3% of total losses ($3,056,000)
- This confirms the typical heavy-tailed distribution of operational risk losses

5. FREQUENCY ANALYSIS
---------------------

5.1 Monthly Frequency Distribution

Month | Count | Total Loss
------|-------|-----------
Jan 2023 | 3 | 185,000
Feb 2023 | 4 | 320,000
Mar 2023 | 5 | 410,000
Apr 2023 | 3 | 195,000
May 2023 | 6 | 520,000
Jun 2023 | 4 | 380,000
Jul 2023 | 2 | 95,000
Aug 2023 | 5 | 450,000
Sep 2023 | 4 | 365,000
Oct 2023 | 5 | 530,000
Nov 2023 | 3 | 275,000
Dec 2023 | 4 | 560,000

5.2 Frequency Statistical Measures
- Mean monthly frequency: 4.0 events
- Median monthly frequency: 4.0 events
- Standard deviation: 1.13 events
- Minimum monthly: 2 events
- Maximum monthly: 6 events
- Frequency trend: Relatively stable with slight upward trend in H2 2023

5.3 Poisson Distribution Fit
- Lambda (λ): 4.0 events per month
- Goodness-of-fit (Chi-square): p-value = 0.32 (acceptable fit)
- Implication: Frequency can be reasonably modeled using Poisson distribution

6. RISK INDICATORS AND TRENDS
-----------------------------

6.1 Key Risk Indicators
- KRI-1: Number of high-severity events (>$100K) per quarter
  - Q1: 3 | Q2: 2 | Q3: 3 | Q4: 4 (increasing trend - AMBER)
- KRI-2: Average loss per event
  - Q1: $76,250 | Q2: $91,250 | Q3: $90,833 | Q4: $113,750 (increasing - AMBER)
- KRI-3: Loss rate per $1M revenue
  - 0.85% (within tolerance of 1.0%) - GREEN
- KRI-4: Number of near-miss events
  - Q1: 8 | Q2: 10 | Q3: 7 | Q4: 12 (stable - GREEN)

6.2 Concentration Analysis
- Top 3 event types by loss: CPBP (28.0%), EDP (26.4%), IF (19.8%)
- These three categories represent 74.2% of total losses
- Mitigation focus should be on these areas

7. MODEL RISK OVERLAY
---------------------

7.1 Scenario Analysis Integration
- Using the empirical loss data, the following scenario parameters are derived:
  - Frequency parameter (λ): 4.0 events/month (Poisson)
  - Severity distribution: Lognormal (μ=10.8, σ=1.35) based on MLE fit
  - Expected Loss (EL): $357,083 per month
  - Unexpected Loss (UL) at 99.9% CI: $1,450,000
  - Stress Loss Scenario: $3,200,000 (based on 2008 financial crisis calibration)

7.2 Model Validation Notes
- Data sufficiency: 48 data points provides moderate confidence (confidence level: 0.85)
- Scenario calibration uses external data overlay for tail events
- Correlation assumptions: Conservative (0.3 correlation between event types)

8. RECOMMENDATIONS
------------------
1. Enhance controls in CPBP and EDP categories (highest loss contributions)
2. Implement real-time monitoring for high-severity risk indicators
3. Increase frequency of control testing for Internal Fraud (high severity, lower frequency)
4. Update scenario analysis parameters quarterly with new loss data
5. Consider capital allocation adjustments based on concentration analysis

9. DATA QUALITY AND LIMITATIONS
-------------------------------
- Data collection threshold: $5,000 (minor losses below threshold not captured)
- Recoveries tracking: 85% complete
- Event type classification: 95% accuracy rate
- Timeliness: 90% of events recorded within 5 business days
- Limitations: Short observation period (12 months); may not capture low-frequency/high-severity events

--- END OF REPORT ---

# IRRBB Behavioral Assumptions Register

## Document Control
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Document Title** | IRRBB Behavioral Assumptions – Pass-through, Core Deposits & Prepayment |
| **Prepared By** | IRRBB Analyst Team |
| **Date** | 2025-01-15 |
| **Version** | 1.0 |
| **Regulatory References** | BCBS 368 (¶¶ 6.33–6.49), EBA/GL/2022/14 (Articles 20–23) |
| **Review Frequency** | Quarterly (or upon material change in product/pricing strategy) |

---

## 1. Pass-Through Rate Assumptions

### 1.1 Definition
Pass-through rate (PTR) measures the proportion of a change in market reference rates that is transmitted to customer rates on non-maturing deposits (NMDs) and loans. PTRs directly affect NII under shock scenarios.

### 1.2 Assumptions by Product

| Product | Upward Shock PTR | Downward Shock PTR | Floor Applied? | Rationale / Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sight / Current Accounts (Retail)** | 25% | 15% | Yes (0% floor) | Retail deposits are sticky; historical regression analysis shows ~20–30% pass-through in rising rates and ~10–20% in falling rates. Floor prevents negative deposit rates. |
| **Savings Accounts (Retail)** | 60% | 40% | Yes (0% floor) | Higher competition and rate-chasing behavior; EBA benchmark suggests 50–70% up, 35–50% down. |
| **Term Deposits (Retail)** | 90% | 80% | No | Contractual rates reprice at maturity; near-full pass-through over a rolling cycle. |
| **Sight / Current Accounts (Corporate)** | 40% | 25% | Yes (0% floor) | Corporate deposits are more rate-sensitive; historical data shows moderate stickiness. |
| **Savings Accounts (Corporate)** | 75% | 55% | Yes (0% floor) | Corporates actively manage liquidity; higher pass-through than retail. |
| **Mortgages (Floating Rate)** | 100% | 100% | Yes (reference rate floor) | Contractual linkage to reference rate; full pass-through by design. |
| **Mortgages (Fixed Rate, new origination)** | N/A | N/A | N/A | New business priced at current market rates; no direct pass-through on existing book. |
| **Corporate Loans (Floating)** | 100% | 100% | Yes (reference rate floor) | Contractual; fully linked to benchmark. |

### 1.3 Justification
- **Methodology:** Historical regression of customer rate changes vs. market reference rate changes over rolling 5-year windows (daily/weekly data).
- **Stress Periods:** PTRs are stress-tested using 2008 GFC and 2022–2023 hiking cycle data to ensure no structural break in behavior.
- **Floor Rationale:** Zero-percent floor for deposits reflects the practical impossibility of negative retail rates in the current jurisdiction and customer expectations.

---

## 2. Core Deposits (Stable Balance) Assumptions

### 2.1 Definition
Core deposits are the portion of non-maturing deposits (NMDs) that are considered stable and unlikely to withdraw under normal or stressed conditions. Only the core portion is treated as non-maturing for EVE purposes; the volatile portion is bucketed to short-term maturities.

### 2.2 Assumptions by Product

| Product | Core Deposit Ratio (Stable) | Volatile Ratio | Replicating Portfolio Maturity | Rationale / Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sight / Current Accounts (Retail)** | 70% | 30% | 5-year (core) / 1-month (volatile) | Based on historical minimum balance analysis (lowest 12-month rolling balance over 10 years). Industry standard range: 60–80%. |
| **Savings Accounts (Retail)** | 60% | 40% | 3-year (core) / 1-month (volatile) | Higher rate sensitivity means lower core; EBA guidelines suggest 50–70%. |
| **Sight / Current Accounts (Corporate)** | 40% | 60% | 2-year (core) / overnight (volatile) | Corporates have higher volatility; operational balances provide some core. |
| **Savings Accounts (Corporate)** | 30% | 70% | 1-year (core) / overnight (volatile) | Highly rate-sensitive; low core ratio. |
| **Sight / Current Accounts (SME)** | 55% | 45% | 3-year (core) / 1-month (volatile) | Hybrid behavior between retail and corporate. |

### 2.3 Justification
- **Methodology:** Historical minimum balance method (rolling 12-month trough) over 10+ years of transactional data.
- **Seasonality:** Adjusted for seasonal patterns (e.g., tax payments, holiday periods).
- **Replicating Portfolio:** Core deposits are mapped to a replicating portfolio of matched-maturity instruments to generate EVE sensitivity. The replicating portfolio tenor is calibrated to the average economic life of the core deposit base.
